pages.text: Some engineering properties of stabilized clayey soils incorporating natural pozzolans and industrial wastes K.M.A. Hossain a,⇑,L .M o l b a Department of Civil Engineering, Ryerson University, Toronto, Canada b Department of Civil Engineering, University of Technology, Lae, Papua New Guinea article info Article history: Received 12 October 2010 Received in revised form 15 January 2011 Accepted 1 March 2011 Available online 26 March 2011 Keywords: Soil stabilization Volcanic ash Cement kiln dust Mechanical properties Durability abstract Clayey soils are stabilized with various dosages of cement kiln dust, volcanic ash and their combinations. The inﬂuence of stabilizers is evaluated through Atterberg limits, standard Proctor compaction, uncon- ﬁned compressive strength, splitting tensile strength, modulus of elasticity and California bearing ratio (CBR) tests. The durability properties of 14 stabilized soil mixtures are also investigated by studying the inﬂuence of water immersion on strength, water sorptivity and drying shrinkage. Correlations between strength, modulus of elasticity and CBR are also established. Developed stabilized soil mixtures have shown satisfactory strength and durability characteristics and can be used for low-cost construction to build houses and road infrastructures. The use of stabilized soils with locally available soils, volcanic ash and cement kiln dust can provide sustainability to the local construction industry. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The natural durability and strength of the soil can be improved through the process of ‘soil stabilization’ using different types of stabilizers. Stabilization of soils with low-bearing capacity is an economical way to strengthen the earth for building purposes [1–4]. Soils can be stabilized by mixing the correct proportion of sandy and clay soil or by mechanical compaction of natural soil that increases the strength and cohesion. Use of stabilizers is not new as natural oils, plant juices, animal dung and crushed anthills have been used for many centuries [5,6]. The oldest record of the use of straw stabilized mud or clay blocks was found in Greece in the Mediterranean dating back to 4600 BC and then spread outward to the other parts of the world [7]. The ‘‘thobe’’ in North Africa and the ‘‘adobe’’ in Central America are well known stabi- lized soil blocks [8]. Scientiﬁc techniques of soil stabilization have been introduced in recent years and the use of cementitious material like Portland cement, hydraulic lime, bitumen, asphalt and certain resins as sta- bilizer is quite common [1,9–14]. Stabilizers with pozzolanic prop- erties can bind soil particles together and reduce water absorption by clay particles. The potential for using industrial by-products/ wastes for stabilization of clayey soils such as blast furnace slag, ﬂy ash, rich husk ash, foundry sand, foundry slag, bottom ash and cement kiln dust is promising and has been investigated [2,15–22]. Stabilized soils can be extremely useful construction materials in many countries of the world, especially if locally available natural/industrial resources are used. Volcanic ash (VA), a natural pozzolan is found abundantly in volcanic areas and cement kiln dust (CKD) is also available as a co-generated by-product of the ce- ment manufacturing process. It is estimated that approximately 30 million tonnes of CKD is generated worldwide each year – approx- imately 80% remains stockpiled or

2. Experimental program 2.1. Collection of materials and their properties Volcanic ash (VA) used in this investigation was collected from the East New Britain province of Papua New Guinea and the cement kin dust (CKD) was supplied by a local cement company. The chemical and physical properties of volcanic ash and cement kiln dust are presented in Table 1 . A locally available clayey soil was collected from the area where volcanic ash deposits are available. The engineering properties of original soil in terms of Atterberg limits as per ASTM D 4318 [25], par- ticle size distribution and soil classiﬁcation are presented in Table 2. Both wet siev- ing and hydrometer methods were carried out in order to obtain an accurate grain size analysis as per ASTM D 422 [26]. Soil classiﬁed as A-6, according to AASHTO (Table 2 ). 2.2. Stabilized soil mixtures and combination schemes for stabilizer Comprehensive series of laboratory tests consisting of standard Proctor com- paction, unconﬁned compression strength, splitting tensile strength, modulus of elasticity, California bearing ratio (CBR), water resistance, water sorptivity and shrinkage were conducted on the selected clayey soil stabilized with various per- centages and combination of stabilizers such as VA, CKD and their combinations. Table 3 presents a summary of stabilized soil mixtures with various stabilizer com- binations. The percentages of VA were 0%, 5%, 10%, 15% and 20%, while the percent- ages of CKD were 0%, 2%, 5%, 10%, 15% and 20%. A total of fourteen combinations based on single and mixed modes of stabilizers were studied. 2.3. Preparation of specimens and testing procedures Initially oven-dry soil was thoroughly hand mixed with each stabilizer or com- bination of stabilizers of in a large tray in a dry state. Thereafter, mixing was carried out in a laboratory mixer for at least 3 min after adding water (required for the in- tended moisture content) and the mix was subsequently put into plastic bags, where the mixing was continued by shaking and overturning the bag for at least 4 min and ﬁnally, the air was squeezed out by hand. The optimum moisture content and dry density for soil with different percentages of stabilizers were determined by the standard Proctor compaction test as per ASTM D 2166 [27]. All test specimens were prepared with the static compaction method as per ASTM D 2166 [27] at the optimum moisture content. The specimens were de- molded 1 min after completion of the compaction and were stored in a curing room (maintained at 23 ± 2 /C176C, 95 ± 2% RH) wrapped in thin plastic ﬁlm until testing at 7, 28, 56 and 91 days. Cylindrical specimens (39-mm diameter and 78-mm length) were used for unconﬁned compressive strength ( S) as per ASTM D 2166 [27] and indirect tensile (splitting) strength tests. Larger cylindrical specimens (75-mm diameter and 150- mm height) were used for the determination of the modulus of elasticity ( E). The compressive and indirect tensile strengths were determined on a hydraulic testing machine under strain-control at a loading speed of 1.14 mm/min. On the other hand, the modulus of elasticity was determined with loading at a constant rate of deformation of 1.0 mm/min (a deformation rate similar to that imposed by trafﬁc on pavement subgrades up to failure in an effort to obtain the stress–strain relation- ship [28]. The CBR test on stabilized soil specimens was conducted as per ASTM D 1883 [29]. The specimens were made in the CBR-mold with the

3.4. Durability characteristics of stabilized soils 3.4.1. Effect of water immersion on compressive strength Table 4 compares the 91-day normal curing unconﬁned com- pressive strength ( S) and those obtained after 7-day water immer- sion (Ss). The effect of the 7-day water immersion on the 91-day compressive strength is presented in Fig. 10 in terms of strength ratio expressed as percentage (100 Ss/S). The ratio increases with the increase in VA and CKD content. In general, for similar dosages of stabilizer, the CKD based soil shows higher strength ratio than that of VA. The strength ratio was greater than 80% for all soil mixes except control (soil), 5 VA and 2 CKD. 3.4.2. Water absorption by sorptivity In general, the single or mix mode of soil stabilization with CKD and VA is found to reduce the 14-day sorptivity substantially. The increase in CKD/VA content seems to reduce the sorptivity of sta- bilized soils ( Table 4 ). The CKD stabilized soils produced lower sorptivity than their VA counterparts. The 14-day sorptivity is re- duced by 59% when VA content is increased from 0% to 20% while a 64% reduction is observed when CKD content is increased from 0% to 20%. The positive effect of reducing the sorptivity of soils with combined chemical (with different stabilizers) and mechani- cal (with compaction by standard Proctor) stabilization can be attributed to the prevention of formation of enlarged pores and cracks due to cementation of the soil particles together, ﬁlling of pore space in the soil and prevention of the reorientation and ﬂoc- culation of soil particles [30]. 3.4.3. Shrinkage Fig. 11 shows a comparison of shrinkage development with time for stabilized soils. Shrinkage rapidly increases during the ﬁrst 7 days for both stabilized and non-stabilized soil specimens and then at later ages, the rate of increase becomes very slow. Hence, curing for the ﬁrst 7 days could be beneﬁcial in reducing drying shrinkage and cracking.. In general, VA stabilized specimens show lower shrinkage compared to their CKD stabilized counterparts. The shrinkage decreases with the increase of VA but increases with the increase of CKD. CKD stabilized soils show higher rate of shrinkage development at early ages (up to 7 days) compared to VA stabilized soils. This can be attributed to the high water de- mand of CKD, the development of higher rate of hydration and the generation of higher heat of hydration at early age. The shrink- age in mixed mode stabilized soils depends on the relative propor- tion of VA and CKD as they tend to compensate each other. Generally, the shrinkage in CKD based stabilized soils can be low- ered by using VA ( Fig. 11 ). Table 4 shows the ﬁnal shrinkage at the age of 91 days for sta- bilized and non-stabilized soil specimens. The 91-day shrinkage is decreased by 32% when VA content is increased from 0% to 20% while an increase of 11% is observed when CKD content is in- creased from 5% to 20%. Overall, the shrinkage of stabilized soils (CKD, VA and their combinations) is found to be lower compared Table 4 Mechanical and durability properties of stabilized soil mixtures. cement content beyond 4% was also conﬁrmed from previous re- search studies [15]. The compaction characteristics depend on both grain size distribution and speciﬁc gravities of the soil and stabi- lizer. The stabilizers initially coat the soils to form large aggregates that consequently occupy larger spaces. Therefore, the tendency of ﬁne-grained soils is to initially decrease the dry density until the stabilizer (which tends to increase the dry density) compensates for the larger spaces. The optimum moisture content decreases and maximum dry density increases with the increase combined dosages CKD and VA in mixed mode stabilization ( Fig. 3 ). The addition of VA and CKD to the soil raises the optimum moisture content and the water demand of CKD is found to be higher than VA. The pozzolanic reac- tion of VA with the soil constituents tends to increase the optimum moisture content. On the other hand, the increase in optimum moisture content with the addition of CKD is attributed to the ex- tra water required for higher ﬁneness and subsequent enhanced hydration. It should be pointed out that the high optimum mois- ture content may be difﬁcult and costly to achieve during dry sea- sons. However, during wet seasons the high water demand of CKD and VA will facilitate compaction. 3.2. Unconﬁned compressive strength, splitting tensile strength and modulus of elasticity Figs. 4 and 5 show the development of the unconﬁned compres- sive strength of stabilized soils with age for various percentages of VA and CKD. In general, the compressive strength increases with the increase of age and stabilizer content. The 91-day compressive strength of mix 20 VA is 3.1 MPa compared to 6.01 MPa of mix 20 CKD. The effect of combining VA and CKD in mixed mode of stabil- ization is shown in Fig. 6. Stabilizer combinations with higher dos- ages produce higher compressive strength. Inﬂuence of CKD content and age on unconﬁned compressive strength. K.M.A. Hossain, L. Mol / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 3495–3501 3497

stabilizers with high CKD produces higher compressive strength. The compressive strength of combination mode stabilized soils ranges between 2.20 and 3.65 MPa. The splitting tensile strength increases with the increase of age and stabilizer content ( Figs. 7–9 ). The CKD based stabilized soils show higher compressive strength than VA based counterparts similar to that observed in the case of compressive strength ( Figs. 7 and 8 ). For single mode stabilization, the maximum 91-day split- ting tensile strengths of 0.43 MPa and 0.59 MPa are observed for Mixes 20 VA and 20 CKD, respectively. For mixed mode of stabil- ization, the 91-day splitting tensile strength ranges between 0.15 and 0.33 MPa. Table 4 presents the 91-day modulus of elasticity ( E) of stabi- lized soil mixtures. E increases with the increase of age and the ef- fect of different stabilizers/their combinations is similar to that observed in the case of strength. For single mode of stabilization, the 91-day E is found to be maximum for Mix 20 CKD (7 GPa) while 5 VA mix produces the lowest E (1.5 GPa). For mixed mode of sta- bilization, the 91-day E ranges between 2.40 and 4.32 GPa. The increase in unconﬁned compressive strength, splitting ten- sile strength and E with the increase of stabilizer contents indicates that the cohesion of the clayey soils increases due to the addition of VA, and CKD. The higher strength of VA and CKD stabilized soil compared to natural soil is due to cementing and pozzolanic prop- erties, respectively. The beneﬁcial effect of combining VA with CKD can be attributed to the transformation of the soil, as VA allows better distribution of CKD and increases its effectiveness. The use of high percentages of VA and CKD, in certain cases, is more effective but the problems associated with the use of large quantities would need to be addressed. These problems include transport costs, practical problems of spreading and mixing these large quantities and increased water demand. However, the study conﬁrms the viability of producing satisfactory stabilized soils with small percentages of VA, CKD and their combinations. 3.3. CBR Table 4 presents the 91-day CBR values for stabilized soil mix- tures. CBR values are found to increase with the increase of age and VA/CKD content (from 0% to 20%). The CKD stabilized soil has much higher CBR values than VA stabilized soil. The better per- formance of CKD and VA stabilized soil can be attributed to the cementing and pozzolanic properties respectively. The results of CBR tests indicate that the appreciable improvement of soil has ta- ken place with all these three stabilizers and their combinations. It should be noted, however, that the 15% minimum CBR value usu- ally required by many speciﬁcations is by far attained even with 5% VA and 2% CKD. Almost all the stabilized soil mixtures produced very high CBR values (>80% – a value that normally characterizes an excellent compacted pavement subgrade) and have the poten- tial to be used for manufacturing bricks for building construction. 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 0 1 42 84 25 67 08 49 8 Age (days) Compressive strength (MPa) 5VA2CKD 5VA5CKD 10VA2CKD 10VA5CKD Fig. 6. to that of non-stabilized (control) soil as particles of such stabiliz- ers oppose the shrinkage movement. 4. Correlation between properties and discussion The relationships between CBR and compressive strength and between modulus of elasticity and compressive strength at 91 day are shown in Figs. 12 and 13 , respectively. A linear relation- ship between CBR and compressive strength as well as E and com- pressive strength exists. Similar linear relationships between CBR and compressive strength have been found for cement-stabilized soils [31]. A linear relationship also exists between CBR and mod- ulus of elasticity ( E) as well as compressive strength and splitting tensile strength (at 91 day) of stabilized soils ( Figs. 14 and 15 ). However, such relationships can be applied strictly to the soil investigated in this study. Generally CKD based stabilized soils showed higher strength/ elastic modulus/CBR/water resistance/shrinkage values and lower water absorption/sorptivity compared to their VA counterparts. This can be attributed to the self-cementing characteristic that al- lows CKD to react with soil in a manner similar to Portland cement. Microstructural analysis conﬁrms that CKD satisﬁes the require- ment for self-cementing characteristics as its hydration modulus is in between that of Alite and Belite [32]. CKD also has approxi- mately one-third of the amount of cement oxides (CaO, Al 2O3, SiO2,F e 2O3) present in Portland cement. 5. Conclusions The following conclusions are drawn from the test results of 14 soil mixtures derived from both single and combination modes of stabilization using volcanic ash (VA) and cement kin dust (CKD) as stabilizers with dosages varying from 0% to maximum 20%: /C15 Stabilized soils exhibit enhanced mechanical properties such as strength (compressive and tensile), modulus of elasticity and California bearing ratio (CBR) as well as durability in terms of water resistance, water sorptivity and shrinkage. /C15 The potential beneﬁt of stabilization is found to depend on the type/amount of stabilizers, stabilizer combinations and the age. CKD stabilized soils showed higher strength/elastic modulus/ CBR/water resistance/shrinkage values and lower water absorp- tion/sorptivity compared to their VA counterparts. The combi- nation of stabilizers with high CKD also produces better mechanical and durability properties and user can try various combination schemes to optimize performance. /C15 Almost all the stabilized soil mixtures produced very high CBR values (> 80%) and have the potential to be used for manufac- turing building blocks for low-cost houses and as subbase layers for road infrastructure for local population. The proposed use of stabilized soils should help promote sustainable development in the construction industry.
